title: Composting in a small garden
subtitle: Setting up and running a single-bin cold compost system
excerpt: A 1 m³ compost bin will process the kitchen and garden waste from a typical terraced or semi-detached house. Cold composting is slower than hot methods but requires far less management. This guide covers bin choice, what to add, and when to use the compost.
sourceNotes: Royal Horticultural Society composting guidance. WRAP Recycle Now garden composting guidance. Garden Organic composting best-practice recommendations.

glossaryTerms.definition: Composting that occurs at ambient temperature (typically 10-20°C). Decomposition is slower (6-18 months) but requires less management than hot composting. Does not reliably kill weed seeds or pathogens.

The ratio of carbon to nitrogen in composting materials. The ideal ratio for active decomposition is approximately 25:1. Too much carbon (browns) slows decomposition; too much nitrogen (greens) causes anaerobic conditions and smell.

Browns are carbon-rich materials (cardboard, straw, dry leaves, woody prunings) that add structure and feed fungi. Greens are nitrogen-rich materials (fresh grass, kitchen scraps, coffee grounds, fresh plant material) that fuel bacterial activity.

Partially decomposed autumn leaves composted separately in a wire cage or bags. Takes 1-2 years to produce but requires no turning. Excellent as a soil conditioner and mulch, though lower in nutrients than compost.

content.text: A
cold compost

bin at 220 litres (a standard subsidised council bin) or 330 litres will handle the waste from a typical household with a small garden. Cold composting takes 6 to 18 months compared with 6 to 8 weeks for managed hot composting, but requires no heat monitoring and minimal attention. A bin on soil is slightly better than on hard standing as it lets earthworms migrate in, but both work.
What to add
The
C:N ratio
determines whether the pile decomposes actively or slowly and smelly. Layer
browns and greens
in approximately equal volumes by weight. A rough guide: for every kitchen caddy of food scraps (greens), add a roughly equal volume of torn cardboard or dry leaves (browns).

Yes: vegetable peelings, fruit scraps, coffee grounds, tea bags (not plastic mesh), grass clippings (in thin layers), garden trimmings, eggshells, cardboard (torn up, not glossy), newspaper, hair, wood ash (small quantities).
No: cooked food, meat, fish, dairy, diseased plant material, dog or cat faeces, persistent weeds (running roots, seeding heads).
Managing the bin
A cold compost bin needs minimal intervention. The main adjustments:
Too wet: the pile becomes slimy and smells of ammonia. Add more brown cardboard or dry material and push a stick through the mass to introduce air. Cover if rain is heavy and sustained.
Too dry: white fluffy fungal strands appear and the pile barely decomposes. Add water with a watering can or add more fresh greens. Cover in dry summer spells to retain moisture.
Flies: cover fresh food scraps with cardboard or a thin layer of garden soil each time you add material. A lid that closes properly keeps large flies out.
Autumn leaves

Autumn leaves are very high carbon and acidic; adding large quantities to the compost bin unbalances it. Instead, bag them separately in black bin bags with a few air holes, or pen them in a wire cage, and leave them for 12 to 24 months to make
leaf mould
. The resulting material is one of the best mulches available and requires almost no attention.
When is it ready

Finished compost is dark, crumbly, and smells earthy. It is usually ready at the bottom of the bin first. Open the hatch or lift the bin and remove mature compost from the base, leaving unfinished material to continue. Apply it to vegetable beds, around shrubs, or as a lawn top-dressing. Partially finished compost can go on beds as a mulch; the remaining decomposition happens in place.

items.cause: Bin is too dry, or ratio is heavily biased toward carbon-rich material
items.fix: Add a bucketful of water and some fresh nitrogen-rich greens (grass clippings work fast). Use a compost aerator or a garden fork to introduce air.

items.symptom: Persistent smell of ammonia or rotten eggs
items.cause: Anaerobic conditions from compaction or too much wet material
items.fix: Add a large quantity of torn cardboard and turn the pile to introduce oxygen.

items.cause: Exposed fresh food scraps on the surface
items.fix: Bury fresh additions under a 50 mm layer of browns (soil or cardboard) every time material is added.
